sections.heading: Prognosis
sections.content: Prognosis for CNS cancers is highly variable depending on tumor type, histologic grade, molecular features, patient age, and performance status. Glioblastoma, the most common malignant primary brain tumor in adults, has a 5-year survival rate around 6%, with higher rates among younger age groups. WHO grade 2-3 gliomas generally have better outcomes, particularly those with favorable molecular profiles. Brain metastases prognosis depends on primary tumor type, number and volume of metastases, patient performance status, and extracranial disease control. Primary CNS lymphoma prognosis has improved with treatment advances. Meningiomas, most of which are WHO grade 1, generally have favorable outcomes with surgical resection. Ependymomas and medulloblastomas in adults are rare but have distinct prognostic profiles based on molecular subtyping.

factors.detail: favorable prognostic marker for OS in grade 2-3 gliomas
confers favorable prognosis and predictive of response to alkylating systemic therapy with or without RT
strong prognostic biomarker and predictive for benefit from temozolomide in high-grade gliomas
associated with IDH mutations; loss of ATRX immunostaining suggests astrocytoma
associated with reduced OS in IDH wild-type gliomas
associated with very poor prognosis regardless of histologic appearance
GTR associated with decreased mortality and lower risk of progression in low-grade gliomas
older age associated with worse prognosis in glioblastoma
associated with grade 4 status in IDH-mutant astrocytomas
WNT-activated associated with markedly better prognosis

factors.factor: Meningioma grade and extent of resection
Brain metastasis volume and rate of developing new metastases
factors.detail: more important than number for prognosis
